Unified Information Architecture
Product Direction
Overview

Your Information Is Everywhere. Finding It Shouldn't Be Hard.

By 2019, Alexa users had accumulated personal data across a growing number of domains — calendar events, reminders, lists, email, contacts, shopping orders. Each domain had its own skill, its own utterance pattern, its own mental model. To find something, you had to already know where it lived.

Research surfaced a clear frustration: users couldn't reliably distinguish between a calendar event and a reminder. They knew they had asked Alexa to remember something — they just couldn't remember what they called it. And Alexa had no way to help them find it across domains.

Alexa Personal Data Search was the answer: a unified, domain-agnostic search experience that let users find any personal information — regardless of where it was stored — with a single natural language query.

5+
Siloed data domains
3
Surfaces designed for
0
Unified search precedents
Echo Show & Echo Spot — "What did I tell you about Amy?" Final UI · Multi-Surface
Both surfaces show the same unified PDS result. The Echo Show renders a scrollable list with domain labels. The Echo Spot adapts to a circular form factor, prioritising the first result and offering scroll for the rest.
The Problem

Three Hard Problems With No Obvious Answers

Core Challenge

Every piece of personal information in Alexa lived in a silo. Calendar, reminders, lists, email, shopping — each had its own data model, its own voice skill, its own interaction pattern. A user asking "Alexa, find the thing about my dentist" could get zero results if they'd stored it as a reminder rather than a calendar event.

There was no concept of unified search in the Alexa ecosystem. No cross-domain query. No fallback when a result wasn't found in the expected domain. Users blamed themselves when the real problem was the architecture.

Problem 01
Each domain was a silo
Calendar, reminders, lists, email, contacts — each lived in its own skill with no shared search layer. Finding information required knowing exactly which domain it was in.
Problem 02
The ordinals question
When multiple results existed, should Alexa say "your first result is..." or just read them straight? Ordinals add structure but also cognitive load. We had no data — we had to test.
Problem 03
No voice search precedent
Visual search has conventions — ranked lists, highlighted matches, filters. Voice has none. We were designing the interaction grammar for personal data search from scratch.
Problem 04
Cross-surface consistency
The same search had to work on a voice-only Echo Dot, an Echo Show with a full screen, and an Echo Spot with a tiny circular display. Three very different canvases for the same result.

"Users knew they had asked Alexa to remember something. They just couldn't remember what they called it — and Alexa had no way to help them find it."

Research & User Insights

Why This Experience Had to Exist

The starting point wasn't a feature request — it was a pattern of failure. Users were already talking to Alexa about their personal data. They asked where their meeting was, when their mom's birthday dinner was, whether they'd added milk to the shopping list. Alexa couldn't answer any of it. The research question wasn't should we build this? It was how broken is the current experience, and how wide does the solution need to be?

01
Users ask in six distinct ways. Research identified five core question types — When, Where, What, Who, Boolean — plus temporal queries ("when did I last…"). Each required a different response shape, not just different content.
02
Personal data lives across four disconnected domains. Calendar events, Reminders, To-Dos, and Remember This content all held answers to user questions — but none of them talked to each other. A user asking "when is street sweeping?" might have the answer in Reminders or To-Dos. Alexa had no way to check both.
03
The same question reaches the same data through many surfaces. "When am I meeting Joe?" could be asked by event title, by attendee name, by location, or by start time. Each entry point required its own utterance mapping — and each had to return the same correct result.
04
Cross-domain collisions were inevitable. A query like "when is mom's birthday dinner?" could surface a calendar event and a Remember This memory simultaneously. The design had to handle ranked results across domains — not just within them.
Scope of coverage

Before design began, the team mapped every question type against every data source. The result: 5+ data domains, 6 question types, 3 result states (single, multiple, zero), and hundreds of utterance variants — all requiring a unified response system that felt like a single, coherent experience to the user.

Sample utterances by question type
When
"When is my doctor's appointment?"
"When is the meeting with Amy?"
"When are recycling days?"
"When did I last take my blood pressure medicine?"
"When am I next seeing the doctor?"
Where
"Where is Matthew's farewell dinner?"
"Where am I meeting Joe?"
"Where did I keep the Christmas decorations?"
"Where is my 2:00 PM meeting?"
"Where do I need to go for my appointment?"
What
"What time is soccer practice?"
"What did I want to buy for Christmas?"
"What is my sister's favorite color?"
"What days are recycling days?"
"What did I want to text Toni about?"
Who
"Who is coming to Matthew's farewell dinner?"
"Who has a peanut allergy?"
"Who did I want to call for Christmas dinner plans?"
"Who is attending the meeting with Amy?"
"Whose birthday is in August?"
Boolean
"Do I have a doctor's appointment this month?"
"Did I put ginger on my shopping list?"
"Is Matthew's farewell dinner sometime soon?"
"Am I meeting Joe today?"
"Are there any bills due soon?"
Temporal
"When did I last meet Steve?"
"When am I next seeing Susy?"
"When did I do my laundry last?"
"When do I need to pay my bill next?"
"When was my last doctor's appointment?"

Source: Use Cases for Personal Data Search, HHO team. Production utterance research across Calendar, Reminders, To-Dos and Remember This domains.

Research hypotheses tested
Hypothesis 1 — Attribution

"Customers do not care about the attribution source for where their personal data is coming from."

Tested with/without domain labels — e.g. "Recycling days are Tuesdays" vs. "I found a reminder: Recycling days are Tuesdays." Attribution won. Users needed to know where the answer came from to trust it.

Hypothesis 2 — Result count

"Customers want only one answer and a hint for other answers when they ask a specific question."

Tested full dumps vs. lead result + offer. Users preferred the best match surfaced first, with a prompt to hear more — reducing cognitive load without hiding relevant data.

Source: Hypotheses — Personal Data Use-Case Prototyping Research, Alexa Multimodal.

North Star Vision

"The real goal wasn't search. It was an assistant that already knew — and told you before you had to ask."

HHO Winning Moments — Starting Your Day · Scene 3 North Star Storyboard
A North Star scenario showing Alexa as a proactive presence — not waiting to be asked, but surfacing what matters at the right moment. Harry didn't search for Timmy's birthday. Alexa knew. PDS was the foundational capability that made this kind of proactive awareness possible.
Design Process

Building a Unified Search Experience

My approach was to design the interaction grammar first — the rules that would govern how results were spoken, how multiple results were navigated, and how zero results were handled — before touching visual design. The voice model had to be right before the screen layer could be built on top of it.

1
Map all personal data domains
Created a comprehensive inventory of every data type a user might want to find — calendar events, reminders, lists, email threads, contacts, shopping orders, notes. Mapped the mental models users had for each, and where those models overlapped or conflicted.
2
Define the unified query model
Designed a cross-domain intent that could route a natural language query to multiple domains simultaneously and surface results in a coherent, ranked order — regardless of which domain contained the answer.
3
Prototype the ordinals debate
Built and tested multiple response patterns — with ordinals ("Your first result is...") and without. Used Keynote conversation prototypes and Adobe XD multimodal prototypes to test with real users on UserTesting.com. The ordinals finding became one of the most debated design decisions of the project.
4
Design the one result vs. no result patterns
Designed distinct response patterns for zero results, one result, and multiple results — each requiring a different conversational shape, different visual treatment, and different recovery path for users.
5
Build the unified visual model
Designed a "Personal Information" unified view for Echo Show — a single card-based display that could render results from any domain in a consistent format, with the source domain labeled but de-emphasized.
6
Redline to the Alexa Design System
Produced complete UX specs and redlines aligned to the Alexa Elements design system — ensuring engineering could implement quickly without ambiguity across all three surfaces.
PDS Decision Flow — 0 / 1 / 2+ results routing Interaction Architecture
PDS acts as a search engine. Depending on result count (0, 1, or 2+), the interaction forks into distinct response patterns. For 1+ results, users can deep-link into the originating domain via touch or voice.
Search List Pattern — 2+ results wireframe UX Spec
The unified result card template — defined once, applied across all domains. The customer's question echoes back as the screen header. Keywords are bolded in results titles. Metadata surfaces source domain, date, time, location, and participants.
Prototype — Search List interaction flow Clickthrough Prototype
A working prototype demonstrating the full search list interaction — from utterance through result display, ordinal navigation, and domain deep-link. Used in usability testing sessions with real Echo Show devices.
UX Redlines — Echo Show & Echo Spot · Amy search results Design Spec · Jan 2019
Final UX spec delivered to engineering — January 2019. Annotated with component references (C1–C3), layout variables, Android attribute values, and responsive breakpoints for sw600 and sw800 screen widths. Produced in collaboration with the GLORIA / Fluid / Bridge design system team.
The Ordinals Debate

To Number or Not to Number

One of the sharpest design tensions in the project: when Alexa returns multiple results, should she number them? Ordinals add navigational structure but also cognitive overhead — saying "your third result" forces users to count mentally. Without ordinals, the list flows more naturally but users lose their place.

We grounded the decision in production data. Systemwide, ordinal-based selection accounted for 17% of all selection utterances — concentrated in music, video, and books, where lists are the primary navigation pattern. Critically, 69% of ordinal utterances were accompanied by a verb ("play the first one," "show me number two"), and 92% of bare ordinal utterances were single-word ("First," "Next," "Last one") — confirming users wanted ordinals as navigation anchors, not as conversational overhead.

We also found that "Alexa, next" ranked fourth across the entire system — meaning users expected to be able to scroll or step through results by position. The shipped design used ordinals conditionally: surfacing them only when 2+ results were returned, and dropping them for single results where the ordinal added no value.

With ordinals — shipped ✓
A: "You have 3 results for dentist. Your first result is a calendar event: Dentist appointment, Tuesday at 2pm. Your second result is a reminder: Call dentist about X-ray. Do you want to hear the third?"
Users could navigate back — "Go to the second one" — without losing context. Especially helpful on voice-only devices with no visual anchor.
Without ordinals — tested, not shipped
A: "You have 3 results for dentist. A calendar event: Dentist appointment, Tuesday at 2pm. A reminder: Call dentist about X-ray. A list item: Dentist insurance card."
Felt more natural for 1–2 results but broke down with 3+. Users couldn't refer back to specific items without re-listening to the whole list.
17%
of all selection utterances used ordinals systemwide
69%
of ordinal selections were paired with a verb ("play the first one")
92%
of bare ordinal utterances were single-word: "First," "Next," "Last one"

Source: Ordinal Data Analysis, Alexa Multimodal — May 2018. Production utterance data across KNIGHT and ROOK device cohorts.

Research Insights

What Users Taught Us

01
Domain blindness is real. Users had no reliable mental model of the difference between a reminder and a calendar event. They just knew they'd "told Alexa something."
02
Zero results was the biggest failure mode. When Alexa found nothing, users assumed they had done something wrong — not that Alexa had looked in the wrong place.
03
Voice and touch needed to work together. On Echo Show, users naturally wanted to tap a result after hearing it. The voice interaction had to anticipate the shift to touch mid-session.
04
Ordinals aid navigation for 3+ results. Below that threshold, they felt formal and unnecessary. The final design used ordinals conditionally based on result count.
Voice Design

Response Patterns & Golden Utterances

Three distinct response patterns were designed — for one result, multiple results, and zero results. Each required its own conversational shape and recovery path.

Response Pattern — Multiple results: "Where is soccer practice?" Visual + Voice · Echo Show
2 results: ordinals surface (1, 2) and Alexa reads both before pausing. The voice response mirrors the visual — same info hierarchy, same structure. The user can say "Go to result 2" or tap to deep-link.
Response Pattern — Single result: "What is the wifi password?" Visual + Voice · Echo Show
1 result: no ordinal needed. Alexa leads with the domain (Memories), states the answer directly, and offers a next step. Clean, no navigational overhead.
Response Pattern — Zero results: "Do I have milk?" Visual + Voice · Echo Show
0 results: Alexa names the domain she searched (shopping list), shifting blame from user to system. A constructive offer follows — never a dead end.
Response Pattern — One result Voice Design Spec
C: "Alexa, find my dentist appointment"
A: "I found one result. A calendar event: Dentist, Tuesday March 12th at 2pm. Do you want me to add anything or is there anything else?"
Single result — no ordinal needed. Alexa states the domain (calendar event), the content, and offers a natural exit or follow-up action.
Response Pattern — Multiple results with ordinals Voice Design Spec
C: "Alexa, what do I have about the dentist?"
A: "I found 3 results. Your first result is a calendar event: Dentist, Tuesday at 2pm. Your second result is a reminder: Call dentist about X-ray. Do you want to hear the third result?"
C: "Yes"
A: "Your third result is a list item: Dentist insurance card, in your health list."
3+ results — ordinals enable navigation. Alexa pauses after 2 results and asks to continue, preventing information overload.
Response Pattern — No results Voice Design Spec
C: "Alexa, find my gym schedule"
A: "I couldn't find anything about gym schedule in your calendar, reminders, or lists. Would you like to add a reminder or create a new list?"
Zero results — Alexa names the domains she searched (shifting blame from user to system), then offers a constructive next step rather than a dead end.
Audio — Alexa PDS response samples Voice Samples · On-device recordings
Query 01
"Alexa, what have I told you about drinking water?"
Query 02
"Alexa, what do I have to do today?" — summary response
Query 03
"Alexa, what do I have to do today?" — full calendar detail
Query 04
"Alexa, what do I have about Joe?"
On-device recordings of the shipped Alexa voice patterns. Queries 02 and 03 demonstrate the same question with two different response strategies — a key design decision around verbosity and how much calendar detail Alexa surfaces unprompted.
Prototype — Reminders domain response Prototype · Voice + Visual
A working prototype demonstrating how PDS surfaces results from the Reminders domain — showing the voice response pattern, visual result card, and interaction flow on Echo Show.
Golden Utterances — what users actually say
"Alexa, find my dentist appointment" "Alexa, what do I have about the meeting?" "Alexa, search for mom's birthday" "Alexa, find the thing about my flight" "Alexa, what did I add about groceries?" "Alexa, look up my car service" "Alexa, find anything about the trip" "Alexa, what do I have this weekend?"
Edge Cases

Designing for the Unexpected

The core happy path was designed early. The harder work — and the work that most shaped the final system — was designing for what happened when things went wrong or got complicated. Two edge cases in particular required dedicated prototyping and testing cycles.

Prototype — Long Memory list navigation Edge Case · Echo Show
When a query returns many Memories results, users need a way to navigate a long list without losing context. This prototype explores scroll behaviour, ordinal anchoring across screens, and how Alexa re-orients the user after a pause in the interaction.
Response Pattern — No Memory found Edge Case · Zero Result · Echo Show
The zero-result state was one of the most consequential design decisions in the project. Research showed users blamed themselves when Alexa found nothing — not the system. The solution: Alexa explicitly names the domain she searched, shifting blame away from the user, then offers a constructive next step rather than a dead end.
Design principle

A zero result is never the end of a conversation — it's an invitation to help the user do something. Every empty state in PDS was designed with a named domain + constructive offer, giving users a clear path forward regardless of what Alexa failed to find.

Outcome & Impact

What Shipped — and What It Changed

Impact

Alexa Personal Data Search shipped in 2019, giving users the ability to find any personal information across domains with a single natural language query — without needing to know which domain it lived in.

The project established the cross-domain search interaction grammar for Alexa — response patterns, ordinal conventions, zero-result recovery paths — that subsequent personal information features were built on.

Most meaningfully, it shifted the mental model: Alexa was no longer a collection of separate skills. She was a unified assistant who knew you — and could help you find anything you'd ever asked her to remember.

The ordinals research became an internally referenced study within the HHO team — one of the few cases where a single interaction decision was tested, documented, and shared across multiple product teams as a design standard.

Reflection

What I'd Do Differently

I would push for longitudinal research on search behavior — not just what users searched for in testing sessions, but what they failed to find over weeks of real use. The zero-result pattern we designed was good, but the data to make it great would have come from real failure cases at scale.

I'd also advocate earlier for a proactive suggestion layer — rather than waiting for users to search, Alexa could surface relevant personal information before being asked. That vision was in the Connect north star document; Personal Data Search was the foundation it needed to exist.
